coredata.dc:title: Controlled carbon dots mixing boosts perylene diimide dye for enhanced absorbance and white light emission: Finding application in optoelectronic devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Surana, Karan
Pansari, Pratibha
Sharma, Roopali
Bhattacharya, Bhaskar
Soni, Saurabh S.
Durga, Geeta
coredata.dc:description: The development of white light-emitting materials is of particular importance for backlights, lighting, and full-color visual display devices. This work successfully attempts to obtain white light by mixing blue-emitting carbon dots (C-Dots) and greenish-yellow-emitting valine functionalized perylene diimide (PDI) dye in an aqueous medium. The two components were mixed in a controlled manner using the host-guest approach, i.e. adding PDI dye to C-Dots and C-Dots to PDI dye for generating C-Dots-PDI composites. The optical characterization of C-Dots-PDI composites revealed intriguing results wherein the incremental addition of C-Dots to a fixed concentration of PDI dye resulted in an unexpected enhancement in the absorbance of the PDI dye. The optimized C-Dots-PDI composites exhibited pure white light emission (CIE coordinates = 0.33, 0.33) upon excitation at 370 nm. The mechanism involved in the interaction was evaluated using photoluminescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and cyclic voltammetry measurement. Additionally, the C-Dots were investigated for their practical application as an invisible ink for transmitting secret messages. Finally, the white light-emitting composites were evaluated for their solid-state emission and also employed as sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC), which revealed the superior performance of PDI as a host and C-Dots as a dopant.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Perylene diimide
Energy transfer
White light emission
Invisible ink
Dye-sensitized solar cell (DSSC)

The authors acknowledge UGC-CPEPA (Phase-II) and UGC-CAS program of the University Grant Commission (UGC), New Delhi for the instrumentation facility.
The author KS is grateful to UGC, India for providing the UGC-DS Kothari Postdoc fellowship (No.F.4-2/2006 (BSR)/CH/20-21/0247).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the C-Dots at a magnification scale of 200 nm, (b) Histogram representation of the C-Dots particle size, (c) HR-TEM image of the C-Dots at a magnification scale of 50 nm, (d) TGA thermogram of the C-Dots.
Under a UV lamp (370 nm) the mixture of (a) C-Dots + xDye (with CD-A under daylight) and (b) Dye + xC-Dots (with Dye-A under daylight).
UV–vis absorption of (a) C-Dots + xDye composites, (b) Dye + xC-Dots composites. The star (∗) marked ones represent white light emitting composites.

PL emission of (a) C-Dots + xDye composites, (b) Dye + xC-Dots composites, (c) individual C-Dots and Dye and their white light emitting mixtures, and (d) CIE color coordinates for CD-E and Dye-H. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
PL decay characteristics of (a) C-Dots + xDye and (b) Dye + xC-Dots. (Inset) Lifetime vs composite plots.
HR-TEM image of (a) Dye-H and (b) CD-E, along with their hypothesized schematic representation.
CV analysis in a two-electrode setup of (a) CD-A and Dye-A, (b) C-Dots + xDye (inset) oxidation region expanded, and (c) Dye + xC-Dots (inset) oxidation region expanded.
Filter paper under (a) daylight, (b) 250 nm UV light, (c) UV lamp (370 nm), (d) after pouring methanol, and (e) pouring water after drying off the methanol.
CD-E and Dye-H emitting white light under 370 nm UV light illumination in solid and gel states.
Photovoltaic characteristics assessment of CD-E and Dye-H sensitized devices (a) J-V measurement and (b) OCVD analysis.
The various C-Dots-PDI composites prepared using different concentrations of C-Dots and Dye.

row.entry:
125 μL (25 ppm)
C-Dots in 3 mL Water

50 μL (10 ppm)
Dye in 3 mL Water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of different materials and methods reported for white light emission using C-Dots.

row.entry: Mixing of RhB dye with C-Dots
Optical thermometry and selective detection of Fe3+ ions
Controlling the dilution ratios between DMF and red emissive C-Dots
Composites of C-Dots and ZnO QD
Controlling the dilution ratios between ethanol of AEAPMS and red emissive C-Dots
Different concentrations of CTAB mixed with C-Dots
Mixing of C-Dots in PDI dye and PDI dye in C-Dots

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: PDMS = Polydimethylsiloxane; RhB = Rhodamine B; DMF = N,N-Dimethylformamide; AEAPMS = N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropyl methldimethoxy silane; CTAB = hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide.

head.ce:title: Controlled carbon dots mixing boosts perylene diimide dye for enhanced absorbance and white light emission: Finding application in optoelectronic devices

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of white light-emitting materials is of particular importance for backlights, lighting, and full-color visual display devices. This work successfully attempts to obtain white light by mixing blue-emitting carbon dots (C-Dots) and greenish-yellow-emitting valine functionalized perylene diimide (PDI) dye in an aqueous medium. The two components were mixed in a controlled manner using the host-guest approach, i.e. adding PDI dye to C-Dots and C-Dots to PDI dye for generating C-Dots-PDI composites. The optical characterization of C-Dots-PDI composites revealed intriguing results wherein the incremental addition of C-Dots to a fixed concentration of PDI dye resulted in an unexpected enhancement in the absorbance of the PDI dye. The optimized C-Dots-PDI composites exhibited pure white light emission (CIE coordinates = 0.33, 0.33) upon excitation at 370 nm. The mechanism involved in the interaction was evaluated using photoluminescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, and cyclic voltammetry measurement. Additionally, the C-Dots were investigated for their practical application as an invisible ink for transmitting secret messages. Finally, the white light-emitting composites were evaluated for their solid-state emission and also employed as sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC), which revealed the superior performance of PDI as a host and C-Dots as a dopant.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The recent progress in artificial lighting and full-color display systems has driven the demand for white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs). Typically, white light is the optimizsed composition of the three primary colors i.e. red (R), green (G), and blue (B) in an appropriate ratio. The most adapted technique to obtain white light is the blending of RGB emitters [1,2]. Nevertheless, such multi-phase light conversion systems suffer from numerous challenges including resorption, poor thermal stability, and sedimentation, resulting in reduced efficiency. Alternatively, rare-earth elements (REs) are used as single-phase phosphor materials, but REs are highly toxic, expensive, and rare [3]. Therefore, the search for newer methods to obtain white light emission led to the development of techniques, such as (a) solvent-induced self-assembly in single dye component systems [4], (b) careful mixing of organogelators [5,6], (c) doping in composite organogels, (d) use of a controlled fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [1,7], such as the interaction of dye and silica nanoparticles (SNPs), and use of fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs), and (e) the interaction of carbon nanoparticles with dye molecules. The development of carbon nanomaterials, particularly carbon dots (C-Dots), and their interaction study with organic dye molecules has been shifting the paradigm for photovoltaic applications [8–13]. The combination of dye with C-Dots often provides a synergistic impact on the device performance, thereby allowing an alternative strategy with color stability, low manufacturing cost, and enhanced environmental safety [14–16].

The C-Dots often called ‘mystic stars’ are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with a diameter of less than 10 nm and have unique size-dependent/independent fluorescence properties [17–19]. Further, they possess good water solubility, remarkable stability, conductivity, low toxicity, and good photostability [20,21]. C-Dots can be synthesized by top-down and bottom-up approaches using an array of precursors and surface passivation agents [22]. Among many methods documented in the literature, the hydrothermal route as a bottom-up approach is the most explored method owing to its low cost, energy efficiency, and easy handling properties [23,24]. One of the differentiating aspects of C-Dots from metal-semiconductor quantum dots lies in the presence of surface defects, which leads to their unique excitation-dependent fluorescence properties [8,25]. These surface defects originate due to the presence of functional groups on the surface of the sp2 hybridized carbon core. These functional groups enable their interaction with other metal quantum dots, drugs, and dyes, thus extending their range of application [9,26,27].

Mandani et. al. studied the interaction of C-Dots synthesized from β-carotene under microwave with Rhodamine-B (RhB) dye and observed white-light emission upon excitation of 365 nm [14]. Yadav et al. explored the lasing behavior of C-Dots/RhB composites with the input laser wavelength of 532 nm by varying pump energy [28]. The C-Dots in this study were synthesized from an electrochemical approach using graphitic anode and cathode with pure water as the electrolyte. Hazarika and Karak synthesized nanohybrid C-Dots using polyester and SiO2 and utilizing them to produce white light by careful mixing with Rhodamine-B [29]. Mousavi et al. used a dual-emission fluorescence system, an amine-carbon quantum dot/rhodamine B ratiometric fluorescent sensor for effective and selective determination of tamsulosin hydrochloride (TMS) [30]. Further, we found that the studies of C-Dots-Dye interactions available in the literature are limited to only a few dyes mainly Rhodamine-B. It was therefore of interest to investigate the effects of a different class of dye on C-Dots, i.e. perylene diimide.

Perylene diimide (PDI) dyes represent a class of fluorophores known for their excellent fluorescence quantum yield (QY), chemical and thermal stability, and tuneable optoelectronic properties [17,31–33]. Since the optoelectronic applications of existing PDI are generally limited by their solubility, we recently synthesized water-soluble valine-functionalized PDI that showed remarkable solubility in other solvents as well [34]. The prepared valine-functionalized PDI showed considerable optoelectronic properties for its potential application in photovoltaics, fluorescence-based detectors, and organic light-emitting diodes (OLEDs).

In the current study, we are reporting the fabrication of C-Dots-PDI composites by using blue emissive nitrogen-doped C-Dots (hydrothermal method) and greenish-yellow emissive valine-ended PDI dye (imidization reaction) [25,34]. The prepared C-Dots were able to function as a security ink. The controlled mixing of PDI dye (hereafter refers Dye) in C-Dots and vice-versa was performed in varying ratios to obtain pure white light emission (CIE 0.33, 0.33). Interestingly, the addition of C-Dots in Dye led to an enhancement in the absorption intensity of Dye. It is speculated that the separation of aggregated Dye molecules and the indulgence of C-Dots as nano emitter centers led to enhanced absorption characteristics. The optimized ratios of white light emitting C-Dots + xDye (C-Dots as host and Dye as dopant) and Dye + xC-Dots (Dye as host and C-Dots as dopant) were utilized as sensitizers in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSC), which revealed that the smaller-sized C-Dots when acting as dopants in Dye resulted in better efficiency. Moreover, the optimized ratios were assessed for solid-state white light emission in different forms. To the best of our knowledge, such an interaction of PDI Dye and C-Dots for the development of white light is being reported for the first time.

C-Dots were synthesized as reported in our earlier publication [25]. Briefly, citric acid and uric acid (1:5) were mixed in double distilled water at ∼ 60 °C. The pH was adjusted to ∼7 by the addition of NaOH following which the solution was subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 20 h in a laboratory oven. The obtained C-Dots solution was successively filtered with a 0.45 μm Millipore filter and 0.1 μm syringe filter and stored in the dark at 4 °C. Since it has been established earlier that only C-Dots are formed owing to the extended hydrothermal treatment time [25], dialysis was not performed for the purification of C-Dots.

The synthesis of valine-ended PDI was also performed as per our previous report [34]. Briefly, perylene tetracarboxylic acid dianhydride and alkalinized valine were reacted in a 1:2 M ratio at 120 °C, which resulted in a dark brown-colored valine-ended PDI precipitate having a good yield (89.49 %). The imidization process was completed in approximately 5 h. PDI was found to be soluble in almost all polar solvents (H2O, DMSO, DMF, C6H6, and so forth) [34]. It is a bola amphiphilic molecule that consists of a small side chain with a hydrophilic –COOH group at both ends, separated by a sufficiently large hydrophobic perylene core.

The absorption, emission, and fluorescence decay analyses were performed in quartz cuvettes having a path length of 1 cm under standard conditions. The UV–vis absorption analysis was performed using a Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer while the photoluminescence (PL) analysis was conducted using a PerkinElmer LS-55 spectrophotometer in a pre-defined wavelength range. The time-correlated single photon count (TCSPC) measurement was measured using a Horiba FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer with an externally mounted Horiba NanoLED pulsed laser diode connected to the TCSPC detector. The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was obtained from a Hitachi STA7200 thermal analysis system under N2 gas flow at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 operated between room temperature to 700 °C. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurement was performed on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation using Pt wires as working and counter electrodes. The measurement was performed at a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 using an aqueous solution of C-Dots or PDI or C-Dots-PDI composite as electrolyte. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed by using a JEOL JEM-2100 microscope at an operating voltage of 200 kV. The diluted aqueous solutions of C-Dots-PDI were thoroughly sonicated and drop-casted on carbon-coated Cu grids. The grids were dried at room temperature for a couple of hours before imaging.

The detailed fabrication process of DSSC is mentioned in our previous publications [17,23]. Briefly, the cleaned FTO glasses were coated with a compact titania layer and a two-scotch tape thick mesoporous TiO2 layer followed by sintering at 450 °C at each step. Pt was also deposited on another FTO glass followed by the usual sintering protocol. For sensitization, the optimized white light-emitting composite of C-Dots and PDI were used, i.e., CD-E and Dye-H (detailed in a later section), wherein the TiO2-coated electrodes were dipped overnight. After pouring the PEO: PEG-based iodine redox couple over the sensitized electrodes [23], they were sandwiched with Pt counter electrodes; thus, completing the device fabrication.

The optical and structural characterization of the prepared C-Dots is shown in our previous report [25]. For assessment of the size and shape of the formed C-Dots, high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) was performed, which confirmed the presence of almost uniform-sized particles having a mean diameter of 13.7 nm with a size ranging between 9.1 and 18.5 nm. The average size greater than 10 nm results from the extended period of hydrothermal reaction, which ensured the presence of only dots (devoid of any other forms of carbon). The prepared C-Dots appearing as dark black dots over the grid background are depicted in Fig. 1 a, which seem to have an almost uniform distribution of particles. The size of thirty particles was estimated using ImageJ software and it is represented in the form of a histogram (Fig. 1b). The HR-TEM image of the particles further confirmed that the particles are free from agglomeration and are of uniform shape as shown in Fig. 1c. One of the particles is depicted clearly in the inset of Fig. 1c having a diameter of 14.6 nm.

The synthesized C-Dots, being organic compounds, require evaluation to ensure their structural stability. Thus, the temperature-dependant degradation study of the C-Dots was performed through TGA analysis. An initial mass loss of ∼4.1 % was observed, which corresponds to the evaporation of water molecules (Fig. 1d). Further, a mass loss of ∼14.06 % is observed up to a temperature of 360 °C, corresponding to the loss of functional groups, such as N and O. Beyond this temperature, breakage of chains and the carbonization of C-Dots occurs. Hence, the prepared C-Dots are quite stable up to ∼360 °C [35].

The characterizations of Valine-ended PDI dye are also detailed in our previous publication [17]. In a nutshell, the Dye exhibits absorption and emission characteristics of a perylene-based Dye with a band gap of 2.31 eV and an absolute quantum yield of 81 % [17].

For assessing the interaction of C-Dots and Dye, their composite was prepared using the host-guest strategy in defined concentration. The stock solution of each of the C-Dots and Dye was prepared using a concentration of 0.6 mg/mL in double distilled water. The various C-Dots-PDI composites were prepared by mixing x μL of Dye as a dopant in a fixed concentration of C-Dots as host (C-Dots + xDye). Alternatively, composites containing x μL of C-Dots as dopants in a fixed concentration of Dye host (Dye + xC-Dots) were also prepared. The various composites prepared are listed in Table 1 . Since both C-Dots and Dye are optically active, they were subjected to absorption and emission analysis for understanding their interaction. It should be noted that the mixing process was repeated thrice, which resulted in similar results each time.

Initially, C-Dots-PDI composite in the aforementioned quantities were analyzed under a long-range UV lamp (370 nm) to visually assess their emission characteristics. CD-A and Dye-A are also represented under daylight, which clearly shows their distinct emission characteristics under UV light. While CD-A is colorless and displays a bright blue color under UV radiation (Fig. 2 a), Dye-A exhibits a brandy color under daylight with bright greenish-yellow luminescence (Fig. 2b). Owing to the exhibition of blue and yellow light emission by C-Dots and Dye, respectively, it is expected that a particular combination should produce white light emission [36]. With C-Dots as host and Dye as dopant (CD-B to CD-K), the color slowly fades before turning pinkish (Fig. 2a). The distinct white luminescence can be observed in CD-E. In the case of Dye being the host and C-Dots as the dopants (Fig. 2b), the greenish-yellow color gradually fades to purple (Dye-B to Dye-K) upon the addition of C-Dots with white luminescence emerging in Dye-H.

The obtained solutions at each stage of UV light imaging were subjected to UV–vis spectroscopic analyses. The absorption of only C-Dots (CD-A) results in a sharp peak at 290 nm with a shoulder peak at 327 nm. As expected, when the concentration of Dye is gradually increased from 0.6 ppm to 6.0 ppm (CD-B to CD-K), an increase in the intensity of its absorption peaks at 500 nm, 535 nm, and a shoulder peak at 470 nm (Fig. 3 a) can be observed while there is no significant rise in the absorption intensity of C-Dots.

Further, the interaction of Dye + xC-Dots was also studied to dive deeper into the light absorption phenomenon of the composite. The Dye (Dye-A) showed its absorption at the same wavelength as observed previously. The addition of C-Dots (Dye-B to Dye-K, from 2.0 ppm to 20.0 ppm) results in an obvious emergence of C-Dots absorption peaks with a gradual and steady rise in its absorption intensity (Fig. 3b). Surprisingly, the addition of C-Dots also resulted in a distinct threefold rise in the absorption intensity of Dye (Dye-A to Dye-K) without any deliberate alteration in the concentration of Dye. This unusual absorbance of the C-Dots-PDI composite may be attributed to various mechanisms such as localized surface plasmon resonance (SPR), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), surface interaction of Dye molecules with C-Dots, light scattering, etc. Since absorption is a molecular phenomenon, it is suspected that upon the gradual addition of C-Dots, the agglomeration of the Dye molecules is reduced. Hence, the rise in Dye absorbance is significantly elevated in the case of Dye + xC-Dots. However, for proper assessment, further understanding of its emission characteristics is required.

Following the absorption analysis, photoluminescence (PL) emission studies of all the solutions were performed. Since the C-Dots-PDI composites were observed under a 370 nm UV lamp, the PL analysis was also performed under the same excitation wavelength. Similar to the results obtained in UV–Vis measurement, as the concentration of Dye is increased in C-Dots (C-Dots + xDye) a gradual increment in the emission intensity of Dye can be observed with only a minor change in the emission of C-Dots (Fig. 4 a). It should be noted that the emission peak at 420 nm corresponds to the emission of C-Dots while the dual peaks at 550 nm and 590 nm correspond to that of Dye. In the case of C-Dots being added to Dye (Dye + xC-Dots), contrary to UV–Vis analysis, a rise in the PL emission of only C-Dots was observed with only a minor change in the emission intensity of Dye (Fig. 4b). A normalized graph showing the individual emission of C-Dots and Dye along with their white light-emitting composite is presented in Fig. 4c. Comparing the change in the emission intensities of Fig. 4a and b, it can be inferred that Dye acting as a host and C-Dots as guests suit their interaction better. It can also be hypothesized that since PL emission is generally a surface phenomenon in the case of carbon compounds, the addition of Dye in C-Dots or vice-versa did not generate any major effect on either entity. Hence, only a minor change in emission intensities of C-Dots and Dye was observed in C-Dots + xDye and Dye + xC-Dots, respectively. Additionally, the calculation for CIE (Commission International de d'Eclairage) coordinates was performed from the PL emission data, and the coordinates obtained for CD-A, Dye-A, and CD-E or Dye-H are (0.16, 0.07), (0.43, 0.55), and (0.33, 0.33), respectively (Fig. 4d). The impact of combining C-Dots and Dye is clearly visible, wherein the optimized composite shows a perfect match for white light emission.

The interaction of Dye and C-Dots is bound to have an impact on their effective fluorescence decay characteristics. Thus, a TCSPC study was performed to assess any relative change in the electron lifetime of the Dye and C-Dots composites. The obtained bi-exponential fitted graphs of the decay analysis of C-Dots + xDye and Dye + xC-Dots are shown in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b, respectively. In Fig. 5a, the obtained lifetime of only C-Dots is 3.44 ns, while in combination with Dye, its maximum lifetime increases to 3.97 ns (CD-E). The marginally extended lifetime could be attributed to the surface passivation of C-Dots by the Dye, which supports the reduction in non-radiative recombination, thereby extending the effective electron lifetime. Similarly, in Fig. 5b, the Dye exhibits a lifetime of 4.70 ns, which is marginally raised to 4.89 ns (Dye-H) in the optimized composite of Dye + xC-Dots.

Similarly, the Dye exhibited a lifetime of 4.70 ns with the maximum lifetime for the Dye + xC-Dots combination extending up to 4.89 ns. In both cases, the higher lifetime was obtained for the white light emitting combinations, i.e. CD-E and Dye-H, thereby further affirming their suitable interaction as observed in prior results. Moreover, owing to the better lifetime of Dye, the Dye-H exhibited a higher lifetime in comparison to CD-E. It could be interpreted that the C-Dots do not impose a major effect on the photo-physics of Dye, leading to a minor rise in the lifetime.

Having performed the absorption and emission studies of C-Dots-PDI composites, it is safe to assume that the interactions do not represent any sign of SPR or FRET phenomenon. It is more likely a surface interaction of C-Dots and Dye molecules, owing to the non-compliance of absorption and emission results. The Dye molecules were assembled through the formation of helical structures and exhibited vesicular supra structures as confirmed through TEM analysis (Fig. 6 ). This type of assembly resulted from intermolecular H-bonding in lateral and π-π stacking in longitudinal growth directions. Taking the case of C-Dots + xDye, as we added Dye into the solution of C-Dots, the C-Dots showed interaction with Dye through H-bonding on the outer surface and then arranged to give a helical (Fig. 6a) arrangement. On the other hand, in Dye + xC-Dots absorption (Fig. 3b), it can be inferred that with the addition of C-Dots, the C-Dots started occupying the cavity of Dye-helical along with the previous arrangement. As more C-Dots enter the solution, the interaction of C-Dots and Dye molecules increases, leading to enhancement in the absorption intensity of Dye (Dye-A to Dye-K). It can be related to the fact that molecules show different absorption characteristics in comparison when the patterns of interactions are different. PL on the other hand, is not affected by this way of C-Dots and Dye integration, thereby the addition of C-Dots has negligible impact on the emission intensity of Dye (since the effective concentration of Dye remains the same). Hence, the separation of aggregated Dye molecules and the indulgence of C-Dots as nano emitter centers results in an effective transfer of energy between the two, leading to enhanced light absorption.

Apart from the optical analyses, C-Dots-PDI composites were also assessed for their electrochemical properties to gain a better understanding of their interactions and possible redox activity. The CV of C-Dots was performed between −1.2 V and 1 V, which depicted a quasi-reversible cycle with an oxidation peak centered at 0.22 V and a comparatively stronger reduction peak at −0.52 V (Fig. 7 a). On the other hand, owing to the presence of only donor species in the Dye, a minor oxidation peak is present at 0.43 V with the absence of any reduction signature. For assessing the interaction of the C-Dots-PDI composite, the potential window was kept the same as that of C-Dots.

In the case of C-Dots acting as a host for Dye (C-Dots + xDye), the reduction peak of C-Dots remained at the same potential with a minor variation in current intensity owing to no change in the effective concentration of C-Dots (Fig. 7b). The observation is consistent with optical analysis, wherein the addition of Dye in C-Dots leads to a minor change in the absorption or emission intensities. The rise in the oxidation peak of Dye can be observed, which is expected with no apparent variation in the oxidation signature of C-Dots. Since the Dye tend to remain in an aggregated state, a shift in the redox peaks from CD-B to CD-K is not observed. Alternatively, as hypothesized from previous CV measurements, the Dye + xC-Dots interaction showed changes in both the oxidation and reduction signatures (Fig. 7c). Since the addition of C-Dots in Dye led to a change in the effective concentration along with the reduction in the agglomerated state of Dye, a rise in the oxidation peak intensity was observed. The occupancy of C-Dots between the Dye molecules and the reduction in latter's agglomerated state led to involvement of a greater number of molecules in the electrochemical process, which resulted in a gradual shifting of the reduction peak. The absence of any additional peak confirms non-involvement of any chemical reaction between the two entities. Thus, the C-Dots-PDI composite entails a physical interaction between C-Dots and PDI molecules involving a reduction in the Dye agglomeration and surface passivation of C-Dots in Dye + xC-Dots and C-Dots + xDye, respectively.

There are limited reports on the application of C-Dots for production of white light by simply mixing with an organic dye. Moreover, the optimization of both Dye in C-Dots and vice-versa provides a strong narrative to the proposed mechanism. A brief comparison of the important reports of C-Dots involved in white light emission is presented in Table 2 .

The practical application of the prepared C-Dots was addressed by applying them on a white filter paper using a paintbrush to spell ‘CARBON DOTS’ and subjected to illumination under various light sources. The C-Dots responded to only long-range UV light (370 nm) with no visibility under daylight and short-range UV light (250 nm) as shown in Fig. 8 (a–c). The C-Dots were unresponsive to other wavelengths too, such as red or green, and were perfectly stable under a wide range of humidity or temperature conditions. Additionally, even upon submerging in alcohol the C-Dots showed no sign of degradation and remained perfectly intact (Fig. 8d). Only upon soaking in water the C-Dots were displaced instantly (Fig. 8e). Hence, the observation supports the application of C-Dots as an invisible ink wherein it can be used to transmit secret messages. The receiver can view the message only under long-range UV light and can discard it easily by splashing water.

The white light emission aspect of the C-Dots-PDI composite was assessed in gel and solid state too. For this purpose, the white light emitting combination (CD-E and Dye-H) was mixed in various concentrations of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to form a thin/thick film and gel. The formed gel and film were visualized under daylight and long-range UV light as shown in Fig. 9 . Interestingly, the polymer was not able to quench the luminescence of the C-Dots-PDI composite in any state and a glowing white light can be observed [41]. Hence, the optimized C-Dots-PDI composite is perfectly suitable for applications involving white light emission in liquid, solid, or gel state.

Finally, the CD-E and Dye-H were applied as sensitizers in DSSC to assess the performance of the optimized composite. The co-sensitization approach towards the use of C-Dots and Dye in DSSC as well as their individual performance has already been explored in our prior publication which revealed a synergetic interaction between the two [15]. Thus, it is expected that their optimized combination will reveal interesting results. The devices prepared using CD-E and Dye-H as sensitizers were exposed to a solar simulator giving an input power of 100 mW cm−2 for assessing their current-voltage (I-V) characteristics. The I–V characteristics of both devices revealed similar characteristics owing to the resemblance in their absorption wavelengths (Fig. 10 a).

The optimized combination of Dye + xC-Dots and C-Dots + xDye would have the same resultant band positions thereby leading to identical open circuit potential (Voc) of the devices (Table 3 ). Since the Dye-H comprised of C-Dots as dopants, which aided in reducing the agglomeration of Dye molecules, a better output current was obtained. Moreover, in Dye-H the absorbance of visible light through Dye is significantly higher in comparison to CD-E (Fig. 3), leading to enhanced conversion of visible light.

The open circuit voltage decay (OCVD) measurement reveals the generation and decay of potential in DSSC in the presence and absence of photons. A swifter decay reveals fast transfer and regeneration of charges through the redox couple and counter electrode. Post 10 s of illumination when both devices achieved the same potential, a similar potential decay characteristic is exhibited (Fig. 10b). The better current generation capability of Dye-H device led to a slight improvement in the fill factor (FF) also, thereby showing superior efficiency in comparison to the CD-E device.

In summary, the current work deals with a comprehensive study on the interaction of carbon dots (C-Dots) and valine functionalized perylene diimide (PDI) dye for achieving white light emission. The controlled mixing of C-Dots and Dye led to white light emission in an optimized composition of C-Dots(25 ppm)+(4.0 ppm)Dye and Dye(10 ppm)+(14 ppm)C-Dots composites with perfect CIE coordinates (0.33, 0.33). Optical absorption and emission analyses revealed that the addition of C-Dots into Dye molecules enhanced their interaction owing to the separation of aggregated Dye molecules and the indulgence of C-Dots as nano emitter centers. The reduction in aggregation was also confirmed by CV measurement. The white light emission upon excitation from a 370 nm light was observed perfectly in liquid, gel, and solid states, which suggests their possible application in OLED and WLED. The aggregation theory was further supported through application as sensitizers in DSSC wherein the Dye-H device generated better efficiency (1.32 %) in comparison to the CD-E device (1.02 %). Additionally, the application of C-Dots as an invisible security ink was also assessed wherein the message written using C-Dots was only visible under a 370 nm UV lamp. It is anticipated that the method reported in this work for production of white light would be useful for production of efficient WLED while the C-Dots and Dye could be applied in organic photovoltaics.

Karan Surana: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Pratibha Pansari: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Roopali Sharma: Methodology, Conceptualization. Bhaskar Bhattacharya: Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Saurabh S. Soni: Investigation, Funding acquisition. Geeta Durga: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Conceptualization.
